verdicts.title: ADR-016 packs post-claim Git closure extraction into spec §11.6's `spawn_grace_s` cold-path watchdog, and fences `run.launch-bind` on it — undeclared deviation
verdicts.reasoning:
The finding's quotes are textually accurate (I verified each against the files rather than trusting any verifier's paraphrase), but the contradiction it alleges is not in the text. Three load-bearing inferences fail.

(1) THE CENTRAL CLAIM — "ADR-016 packs post-claim work into spawn_grace_s" — misattributes authorship of the bound to the ADR. Spec §11.6:326 defines the watchdog as covering "the post-commit gap", and spec §10:334 states the resident effects folder/run.json/proxy/container AFTER the lock is claimed and stamped (acquired_at) and BEFORE the runner's first heartbeat. Under Inv. 2 (decide-then-effect) every resident effect is by construction inside the acquired_at→first-beat window. The spec, not ADR-016, puts setup inside spawn_grace_s. ADR-016 Decision 3's `min(15 minutes, remaining spawn_grace_s)` abandon rule restates that spec-imposed bound and adds exact cleanup ("Timeout performs exact cleanup rather than starting stale work") — strictly more fail-closed. Decision 6's launch-bind fence sits at create→inspect→start, strictly EARLIER in wall-clock than the first heartbeat the spec already gates on the same deadline; it therefore admits no run the spec's own watchdog would not also reap. It fails earlier and cleans up. That is the AGENTS.md §6 definition of conservative, not a deviation.

(2) "no repository work is contemplated inside it" is the reviewer's inference, not spec text. §11.6:544 opens "The thresholds map cleanly onto container realities" — a descriptive mapping gloss, not a closed exclusion list. It never says "only". Decisively, its own enumerated scope already contains an unbounded operation: "image pull". Per AGENTS.md environment facts the arm64 base image carries a ~1–2 GB Playwright browser, so a cold pull can exceed 60s on the spec's OWN listed scope. Any tension between "absorbs image pull" and a 60s default is pre-existing in the spec; ADR-016 neither originates nor contradicts it.

(3) "the ADR's own 15-minute arm is dead code" is affirmatively false. §16 opens "Everything in this section is **tunable policy, never invariant**"; §16.2 says "Shipped defaults, compiled into `mc` — the values in the tables below"; §16.3 heads the table "**Timing** (`config.toml [timing]`)" with a Default column. spawn_grace_s = 60 is a tunable default, not a constant, so min(15 minutes, remaining spawn_grace_s) is well-formed across the knob's range and the 15-minute arm binds for any operator value above 15m.

(4) The charging complaint cites spec-conformance as the contradiction. ADR-016's "charged only by the ordinary spawn-grace reaper on a later tick" is spec §10:338 verbatim (reap → decrement dispatch_retries → block at zero, "never a silent loop"). That is the spec's designed handling of infra death, not a defect the ADR introduces.

(5) The absence of an IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES entry is immaterial once no deviation exists. The added Git *weight* comes from ADR-017, and that IS already declared (IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md:817, "Standalone tasks use a sanitized task-local Git repository", expressly citing ADR-017 Decisions 5–6).

WHERE I LAND AGAINST THE THREE VERIFIERS: A and B are correct. C confirmed at minor on two grounds that do not hold — "dead code at the shipped default" (refuted by §16's tunability text; the ADR expression is well-formed for every legal value, and an unreached branch at one default value is not a textual defect) and "semantic drift from failed-spawn detector to work budget" (refuted by §11.6:326's explicit "post-commit gap", which already makes the window cover host-side post-claim work). C's own reasoning concedes both that §11.6 is not an exhaustive prohibition and that the throughput claim is unverifiable, which leaves its confirmation without support.

MINOR CITATION DEFECT IN THE FINDING: the closure-extraction text it attributes to ADR-017 "Decision 5" actually sits in ADR-017 Decision 6 (deterministic destinations, ~line 328+); Decision 5 covers the protected set and jurisdiction.

WHAT REMAINS (not the finding as stated): the empirical worry that spawn_grace_s=60 may not cover ADR-017's closure extraction plus create on VirtioFS is real and worth measuring — but it is a prediction about throughput, not a claim any ADR or spec text makes, it is a property of the spec's own default which already had to absorb an image pull, and it is remediable by a knob the spec deliberately exposes. As framed — "major, undeclared deviation in ADR-016" — the finding does not survive the text.
verdicts.evidence:
DECISIVE REFUTING TEXT — the spec itself places post-claim resident work inside the grace:

specs/mission-control-spec.md:326 (§10 crash detection): "- **First-heartbeat watchdog (`spawn_grace_s`).** A *stamped-but-never-heartbeated* slot is a failed spawn (the post-commit gap, or a cold-start that never came up); reaped in seconds, not after ~75 min."

specs/mission-control-spec.md:334 (§10): "When that committed decision requires host authority to realize — creating the session folder, materializing `run.json`, wiring the proxy, starting or stopping a container — the `mc` invocation returns **structured effect data** to the resident... The resident effects it in order — folder, `run.json`, proxy, container — so the mount target always exists before the mount (§11.3)."
(The lease is claimed and stamped at commit; every effect above is the "post-commit gap" of :326. Setup was already inside spawn_grace_s by the SPEC's construction.)

REFUTING TEXT — §11.6's clause is a descriptive gloss containing its own unbounded operation, not a closed exclusion list:

specs/mission-control-spec.md:544: "The thresholds map cleanly onto container realities. `spawn_grace_s` absorbs the container cold path — image pull, proxy wiring, `docker run` startup — and a lock slot that was stamped but never produced a first heartbeat is a failed container spawn, reaped in seconds."
(The word "only" appears nowhere; "image pull" is itself arbitrarily long. AGENTS.md environment facts: "the Playwright browser adds ~1–2 GB to it" — the spec's own listed scope can already blow 60s.)

REFUTING TEXT — "dead code" is false; the value is a tunable default:

specs/mission-control-spec.md:690 (§16 preamble): "Everything in this section is **tunable policy, never invariant** — the mechanisms of §1–§15 hold for any legal value of any knob here."
specs/mission-control-spec.md:717 (§16.2): "**Shipped defaults**, compiled into `mc` — the values in the tables below."
specs/mission-control-spec.md:745/750 (§16.3): "**Timing** (`config.toml [timing]`): | Knob | Default | ... | `heartbeat_interval_s` / `spawn_grace_s` | 60 / 60 |"

REFUTING TEXT — the charging path the finding calls the defect is the spec's own:

specs/mission-control-spec.md:338 (§10): "reap: mark the run reaped, decrement the subject's `dispatch_retries`, free the lease... At `dispatch_retries = 0`, set `blocked` with the reason instead (never a silent loop)."
vs. docs/adr/016-boundary-plan-handshake.md:795-796: "It leaves the claimed lease without a heartbeat and is charged only by the ordinary spawn-grace reaper on a later tick." (a restatement, not a departure)

ADR-016 AS ACTUALLY WRITTEN — fail-closed derivation, not invention:

docs/adr/016-boundary-plan-handshake.md:350-355: "Landing has a fixed 15-minute foreground wall deadline. Pipeline setup is bounded by `min(15 minutes, remaining first-heartbeat spawn_grace_s)` and must abandon before that grace expires; Homie setup is bounded by `min(15 minutes, remaining homie_idle_timeout)`. Timeout performs exact cleanup rather than starting stale work."

docs/adr/016-boundary-plan-handshake.md:736-740: "the resident invokes private host-scope `mc run launch-bind` with the exact run, plan digest, and Docker id. Under `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` it CASes the still-held same-run lease and matching Run/plan with no bound id, requires lock-domain `now` to be strictly before both `acquired_at + spawn_grace_s` and the hard deadline, then stores the id/time."
(This fence sits at create→inspect→start — strictly before the first heartbeat the spec already gates on the same deadline. It rejects nothing the spec would have admitted.)

docs/adr/016-boundary-plan-handshake.md:785-788 (ordering chain, quoted accurately by the finding): "persistent-own-folder validation → trace/attachment reconciliation → ephemeral launch projections → setup → gateway/guard → agent create → complete host trust recheck → inspect → pipeline `run.launch-bind` or Homie `homie.launch-bind` receipt → start."

THE ADDED WEIGHT IS ALREADY A DECLARED DEVIATION:

IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md:817: "## 2026-07-13 — Standalone tasks use a sanitized task-local Git repository" — "Where: Phase-3 committed-state mount review; spec §5, §6.2, §7, §11.1; ADR-016 Decisions 5–6; ADR-017 Decisions 5–6".

CITATION DEFECT IN THE FINDING — the closure-extraction text is ADR-017 Decision 6, not Decision 5:
docs/adr/017-mount-authorization.md:328-333: "Fixed plumbing walks the complete object closure reachable from the pinned commit and streams it into a new task-local pack... The helper verifies every object hash/type and proves that the new store's object set is exactly that closure before it creates any ref."

verdicts.title: ADR-017 converts spec §11.3's RW `/workspace` scratch root into an RO mode-0555 bind for standalone-task roles — undeclared deviation
verdicts.reasoning:
The finding's quotes are all verbatim-accurate and the contradiction is real, but it is a ledger/documentation gap rather than a design defect.

1. CONTRADICTION IS TEXTUAL AND DIRECT. Spec §11.3's mount table (line 457) fixes `/workspace` as host-source "container-local scratch", access "RW". ADR-017 Decision 6's destination table (line 502) makes `/workspace` "exact operator-owned mode-0555 task-local skeleton root, always RO" for standalone-task roles. Both the host-source column (container-local scratch vs. bind of a host path) and the access column (RW vs. RO) diverge. Decision 5 makes this deliberate and non-recoverable: "The task root ... is operator-owned mode 0555 ... Thus uid 10002 cannot chmod the parent or create a sibling."

2. THE DEVIATION IS GENUINELY UNDECLARED. Tested the two strongest defenses; both fail on the text:
 (a) Delegation defense: ADR-017's header claims "Where the spec delegates: §§5, 11.1-11.3, 15.3, and 16 require an operator-owned allowlist ... without fixing their grammar or collision-free container destinations." The delegation is scoped to grammar and destination naming. §11.3's table does not leave `/workspace`'s access mode unfixed — it states RW. Flipping a spec-fixed table row is not within the delegated scope, so docs/adr/ is not the correct home under AGENTS.md §6.
 (b) Already-declared defense: ADR-017 line 413 DOES carry an explicit deviation declaration, but scopes it to "§§6.2, 7, and 11.1" and concerns only where the branch lives pre-approval. The IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md entry "2026-07-13 — Standalone tasks use a sanitized task-local Git repository" scopes to "spec §5, §6.2, §7, §11.1"; its Choice text ("keep the standalone branch/worktree in an isolated task-local repository") and Spec-impact text ("§§6.2, 7, and 11.1 should distinguish...") change only branch location. Neither mentions §11.3 or the RW→RO change.
 (c) Exhaustive check: grep for "/workspace" and "0555" across all 909 lines of IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md returns ZERO hits. No entry anywhere in the file mentions the `/workspace` row. This is presence-of-text evidence, not failure-to-find.

3. THE FINDING'S CHARACTERIZATION IS ACCURATE, NOT A MISREAD. The reviewer correctly scopes the change to standalone-task roles (Decision 6's Presence column: "standalone-task roles use the bind; every other agent gets only the structural rootfs directory" — non-standalone agents get a writable image-rootfs dir, matching the spec). The reviewer also correctly notes Packager/Refiner retain the nested artifact children.

4. SEVERITY IS MINOR, NOT MAJOR (departing from the finding's own charge). The change TIGHTENS confinement and preserves the fail-closed posture — it is the mechanism enforcing §5's committed-state isolation. It breaks no invariant (§11.3's table is not an invariant; Inv. 22 scoped jurisdiction is strengthened, not weakened). Roles retain writable ground at `/workspace/artifacts/<target>/<suffix>` ("bilateral mode (normally RW)"), `/home/agent` (RW synthetic home), and `/mc/records/output` (RW). A grep confirms the RW-scratch purpose string "staged outputs, evidence captures" appears exactly once in the entire spec/ADR/contract corpus — nothing else depends on it. Per AGENTS.md §6, "Log-and-go is the default. An entry is informational unless the gap needs operator-only input or breaks an invariant." Neither trigger fires.

DISPOSITION: append an IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md entry covering spec §11.3's `/workspace` row (Gap: §11.3 pins RW container-local scratch; the 0555 RO task-root bind that §5 isolation requires cannot also be an agent-writable scratch root. Choice: standalone-task roles take the RO bind and use artifacts/home/records for scratch; Spec impact: §11.3's `/workspace` row should distinguish the standalone-task RO task-root bind from the ordinary RW scratch dir). No code or ADR change needed — the design is sound and conservative; only the deviation log is incomplete.

verdicts.title: ADR-017 mandates an initiative/child refusal that ADR-016's closed consequence table cannot express
verdicts.reasoning:
Read probe succeeded (/Users/vinchenkov/Documents/dev/ai/homie; spec 829 lines, ADR-016 965 lines; ADR-017 read in full at the cited ranges).

The finding's load-bearing textual claims all check out against the actual files:

1. ADR-017 really does mandate a refusal for this candidate shape, in two places: line 446-450 ("This ADR accepts no initiative mutable-worktree or Git-control arm; an initiative/child candidate needing that path is not eligible for the accepted Phase-3 spawn path...") and the acceptance section, line 1097-1099 ("An initiative/child candidate that needs the parked shared worktree is refused as unsupported rather than receiving a standalone worktree, committed projection, or live primary fallback"), plus line 1101-1103 asserting the structural-plan table proves "fail-closed initiative/child refusal". So the refusal is a required, tested plan-tier outcome, not incidental prose.

2. ADR-016 line 544 really does say "The v1 consequence classes are closed:" and its three-row table enumerates every stable code. I read all three rows. No code names an unsupported/parked candidate shape. ADR-016 contains no occurrence of "initiative" or "wave" at all (grep returns nothing), so nothing elsewhere in it carves out this case, and its only refusal universe is Decision 4's closed set — Decision 3 (line 66) has the broker send "exact plan or classified refusal" and the helper "applies exactly the reselected candidate's consequence. It never falls through to another candidate."

3. ADR-017's own "Stable rejection codes" block (lines 998-1016) is exactly the fifteen `mount.*` codes the finding lists, ending "Every code aborts the whole plan; none drops one mount." No unsupported-shape code there either.

So the mandated "refused as unsupported" outcome has no representable stable code in either ADR's closed enumeration, and ADR-017 never assigns an existing `mount.*` code to it. ADR-017 Decision 4's grant list (lines ~189-214) is itself closed and simply does not contain a shared initiative worktree, so the planner would not naturally emit a rejected mount for it — the refusal would have to come from somewhere the texts do not define.

Reachability is real, not hypothetical: IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md line 517-519 states "nothing gates Editor promotion of an `initiative`-scope proposal, so dispatch will spawn Strategist(initiative)", and spec §6.1/§7 require that role to work in the shared worktree ("Promoting an initiative cuts **one branch and one shared worktree** off main"; "Strategist(initiative)'s first act at every wave boundary is merging main into the branch"). So a candidate ADR-017 says must be refused can be selected today.

Two qualifications that pull severity down (and away from the reviewer's framing, not from the core claim):
- The reviewer's "liveness wedge via `preflight.stale` every tick" arm is inference, not text — nothing in either ADR says the plan yields no code; an implementer could reuse a `mount.*` code (the reviewer's own second arm) and get a task block, which does not wedge dispatch.
- The wave verb is parked and not CLI-wired (PROGRESS.md line 47; IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md line 521-522 "`strategist wave` is not CLI-wired, so no child can bypass Editor review"), so the *wave-child* path specifically is unreachable; only the Strategist(initiative) path is.
- I grepped mc/ — no shipped code implements an initiative-unsupported refusal, so this is purely a design-document coherence gap.

The existing note "A promoted operator initiative dead-ends while the wave verb is parked" (IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md line 514) covers Editor promotion and the zero-wave arc packet; it says nothing about the boundary-tier refusal code, so the finding is correct that it does not already declare this deviation.

Confirmed as a sibling-ADR coherence defect: ADR-017's text requires a refusal that ADR-016's explicitly closed table cannot classify. Minor — it needs either a stable code (or an explicit assignment to an existing `mount.*` code) or an explicit statement that §10 selection filters the shape out before prepare.

verdicts.title: The `mc-probe-mount-<p12>` canary's network mode is unpinned across all four ADRs while ADR-019 forbids Docker's default bridge and its acceptance inspects network mode on all five probes
verdicts.reasoning:
The finding's conclusion — "an acceptance row that cannot be written because no ADR fixes the value" — is not supported by the text, for two independent reasons.

(1) The finding's load-bearing premise is factually wrong. It asserts Decision 3's `container:<their-guard>` names "a guard that does not exist at preclaim". ADR-018 Decision 8 opens by creating exactly such a guard for every candidate: "Every spawn/wake candidate gets exactly one temporary guard and two separate capless started clients, plus the never-started mount canary and the Git-setup canary below: five containers total", named `mc-probe-guard-<p12>`. ADR-018 also establishes the probe-context substitution pattern explicitly — probe containers do not reference a production guard, they reference the probe guard: "Each network client uses `NetworkMode=container:<probe-guard-id>`". So `<their-guard>` resolves at preclaim to `mc-probe-guard-<p12>`; it is realizable, not dangling.

(2) ADR-019 Decision 3 does fix the canary's network mode, by class. ADR-019's acceptance paragraph is precisely a class-assignment for the preclaim objects — "ADR-018's preclaim objects are not an exception" — and maps the mount canary to the candidate's `pipeline` or `homie` class. The same paragraph requires tests to inspect "every hard/resource/security field on all five containers"; the canary's security fields (uid 10002:10002, `CapDrop=ALL`, NNP-absent) have no source other than Decision 3's pipeline/Homie row, and that same row's governing paragraph carries the network line. Class membership therefore imports the network rule along with the security posture; the value is `container:mc-probe-guard-<p12>`. Decision 3's closing sentence "No class silently receives Docker's default bridge" is the rule that forecloses the default-bridge outcome the finding predicts, not evidence for it.

The reviewer's real observation is that the word used in both ADR-018 ("carries that same candidate tuple") and ADR-019 ("use the selected candidate's `pipeline` or `homie` tuple") is "tuple", and Decision 1 defines a tuple narrowly as three integers. That is a genuine drafting imprecision worth a clarifying clause, and it is the strongest residual here. But it does not survive as a defect: the acceptance sentence demands security-field inspection on the canary too, which only class membership can supply, so reading "tuple" as excluding Decision 3 would also leave the canary's uid and CapDrop unfixed — a reading the acceptance text itself rules out. No contradiction with the spec or an invariant exists; the spec supplies no network-mode text at all (no hits for "network mode" or "default bridge" in either spec file), so this is entirely ADR-internal. IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md declares no deviation here, and no Go code under mc/ references NetworkMode or the mount probe, so nothing shipped depends on the ambiguity.

The guard alone joins a deterministic isolated bridge. Each network client uses `NetworkMode=container:<probe-guard-id>` and shares only the guard's network namespace"

It carries the same managed/component/prepare-token labels plus ADR-018's closed `mc-probe-role=mount`, no tier, no launch credential, no command capable of reading the binds, and is never started."

verdicts.title: Ledger's "two explicit spec deviations" undercounts what 20a1a50 actually logged (both claimed entries verified present)
verdicts.reasoning:
Read probe succeeded cleanly (/Users/vinchenkov/Documents/dev/ai/homie; spec 829 lines, ADR-016 965 lines; no flapping).

The finding's core claim is factually correct and, if anything, understated. Commit 20a1a50 ("phase3: accept boundary architecture ADRs") appended EIGHT `##` entries to IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md (+155 lines), while the PROGRESS.md entry written in the same commit says "The two explicit spec deviations—pre-landing task-local Git and one stale-writer cleanup before Console/landing—are appended to `IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md`". Two named vs. eight appended. An auditor working from the ledger alone would look for two and miss six.

One correction to the finding's reasoning, which does not change the verdict: the finding treats the presence of a "Spec impact:" line as the marker distinguishing a deviation from an informational entry. That inference is wrong — `Spec impact:` is a MANDATORY field in the entry template at the top of IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md, with "none" as a permitted value (the 2026-07-10 OPERATOR-INPUTS entry uses `Spec impact: none`). So the line's presence proves nothing by itself. However, the conclusion survives by a stronger route: (a) the file is titled "IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES — the deviation log" and scoped to "every judgment call the agents made that the spec didn't cover", so everything in it is a deviation by the file's own definition; and (b) all eight of the new entries carry NON-"none" Spec impact values, each proposing concrete spec text changes (§11.4, §15.3, §11.5, §15.4/§15.5, §15.4, §15.3, §§6.2/7/11.1, §10).

I also tested the most charitable reading — that "explicit spec deviations" might mean "deviations from explicit spec text" as opposed to gap-filling where the spec is silent. That reading still undercounts. At least two of the six unnamed entries contradict explicit spec text: the `open+audit` control-address floor narrows §11.4's literal table row "everything passes" (spec line 485), and "Helper uses a component label, not an agent tier" directly overrides §11.5's explicit "It carries the mc-tier labeling like every other container" (spec line 516). Both were verified against the spec text, not inferred from the ADR.

No ADR content is at fault and no invariant is broken — this is purely an AGENTS.md §5 ledger-accuracy defect ("Update PROGRESS.md after every meaningful step... It is the only cross-session, cross-harness memory"). The undercount matters precisely because the ledger is the sole cross-harness memory: six load-bearing Phase-3 deviations are invisible from it.

verdicts.title: ADR-016 gates the lease-free Homie tier behind the pipeline lease, breaking Inv. 1, §11.6 and §15.5
verdicts.reasoning:
Read probe passed cleanly (829 / 965 lines, no flap). I read ADR-016 Decision 3 (L304-425), spec Inv. 1/3 (L18/L20), §10 (L330-345), §11.1 (L433), §11.6 (L545-550), §15.3-15.5 (L625-675), and IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md L839-855 directly.

The finding's DESCRIPTION of the mechanism is accurate — ADR-016 branch 1 does place Homie wake/idle-end/exit-reconciliation behind `Fresh -> return`, and the ADR defends this knowingly in Alternatives rejected. The reviewer did not misread the ADR. But the finding's DISPOSITIVE CLAIM — "breaks Inv. 1, §11.6 and §15.5" / "the spec is explicit that the pipeline's lease must never govern the Homie tier" — is refuted by the text.

1. THE FINDING'S LOAD-BEARING PREMISE IS INVERTED BY §15's OWN WORDS. Verifiers A and C both hinge on "§10's table contains no Homie branch at all; §15/§11.6 are the more specific text governing the Homie tier." But §15 does not carve Homie out of §10 — it points AT §10, twice, for exactly the action in dispute. §15.5: "the resident's next timer tick wakes a Homie container if one is not already warm (§10)". §15.3: "`mc dispatch` (single call site is the resident's tick, **for everyone**, §10)". Homie wake IS selected by the §10 dispatch tick, by the spec's own routing. And Inv. 3 — a numbered invariant, stronger than the prose cited against it — says `mc dispatch` "runs only when the global execution lease is free." ADR-016 branch 1 is the literal reading of Inv. 3 + §10 step 0, not a contradiction of them.

2. VERIFIER C's COUNTER FAILS ON CONTEXT. C leans on §15.4's "Resume holds no lease and is not a dispatch (Inv. 2 untouched)" to argue §10 cannot govern Homie. But the parenthetical fixes the meaning: it is the same construction as §15.3's "Operator actions only write state; they never dispatch (Inv. 2 untouched — the next tick picks up the result)." It says the resume VERB does not itself dispatch; the next tick effects it. That is precisely §15.5's model, and it routes through §10. C's reading would make §15.4 contradict §15.5 two paragraphs later.

3. Inv. 1 IS ACCOUNTING, NOT SCHEDULING. Its text: Homie sessions "never count as pipeline runs," under the heading "At most one leased pipeline role run exists." ADR-016 has Homie take no lease and count against nothing. Being scheduled after a lease check is not holding a lease. Not broken.

4. §11.1/§11.6/§15.4's "never against the lease" IS A CRITERION RULE, AND ADR-016 PRESERVES IT VERBATIM. §11.1 says "the lease is never the liveness TEST for Homie sessions." §11.6's surrounding sentences make the referent unmistakable: "A `pipeline` container holding no live lease is stopped — the lease is exactly its liveness... the tier label keeps the lease-keyed rule from REAPING the one lease-free tier." The rule forbids using lease-absence as the kill criterion for homie containers. ADR-016 branch 3 classifies Homie liveness by "an active registry row"; branch 6 by "`homie_idle_timeout`, ordered `(last_activity_at,id)`." No lease criterion appears in any Homie branch. A scheduling precondition is not a liveness test.

5. §15.5 IS NOT AN SLO. "the tick latency is paid only on a session's first message or after an idle-out" contrasts WHICH OCCASIONS pay tick latency (cold vs. warm-within-exchange). Decisively, Inv. 3 asserts lease-gating and one-tick conversation-wake latency in the SAME SENTENCE without treating them as conflicting — so the spec's author does not read these as contradictory.

6. §15.3's "holding the global lease would freeze all real work" forbids HOMIE HOLDING the lease. ADR-016 does not. The "same freeze in reverse" is rhetoric, not text. And the one direction of protection §11.6 actually states — "an open operator conversation must never block pipeline dispatch" — is preserved, not inverted.

WHAT IS REAL (narrow, and not the finding as framed): (a) §11.6 says "Orphan cleanup runs at every tick start"; under ADR-016 branch 3 sits behind branch 1, so it is skipped on a fresh-lease tick. IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md already logs an adjacent ordering deviation quoting this same clause, but it covers only cleanup-vs-Console/landing and explicitly presupposes lease reconciliation runs first ("after lease/recovered-health reconciliation"). It does not cover the fresh-lease skip. (b) The cold-wake latency under a long healthy lease is a knowingly-chosen consequence worth recording. Neither breaks an invariant, so per AGENTS.md §6 this is log-and-go, not fix-red-first. Note the spec IS arguably in tension with itself here (§15.5 routes wake through a §10 whose step 0 returns on a fresh lease), which is why the ADR had to resolve something — but it resolved it toward the spec's literal text and its numbered invariant, which is the conservative choice under AGENTS.md §6, not against them.

Downgrading "major / breaks Inv. 1, §11.6, §15.5" to a logged deviation entry covering the §11.6 tick-start skip and the latency consequence.
verdicts.evidence:

verdicts.title: ADR-017's mode-0700 setuid-owned seal and attachment roots have no creation mechanism on the macOS target
verdicts.reasoning:
Read probe clean (829 spec / 965 ADR-016 lines, no flapping); ADR-017 (1220 lines) read directly.

THE NARROW CORE IS TEXTUALLY TRUE. ADR-017 requires two host-side MC_HOME bind *sources* to be owned by the container-side privileged uid (10001) at mode 0700: `MC_HOME/seals/<run-id>/` (Decision 4 L193-195; mount table L530) and `MC_HOME/attachments/<session-id>/out` (table L532; Decision 8 L949-951). Ownership, not mode, is load-bearing — 0700 only denies uid 10002 if the owner is 10001. No text in ADR-016/017/018/019, specs/, docs/phase3-contract.md, or IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md names an actor or step that establishes that ownership. `grep -rni "chown|uid map|idmap|uid remap|sudo|root phase"` over docs/adr/*.md, specs/*.md, phase3-contract.md returns exactly three relevant hits: ADR-019:187 which *denies* the mechanism ("there is no root agent, host `chown`, or permission-widening fallback"), phase3-contract:175 which asserts "no uid remap/ECI" as a *canary requirement*, and spec:554 which names uid remapping as a *failure cause*. So the ADR family positively closes the door on the two obvious fillers rather than merely omitting them.

VERIFIER B'S REFUTATION FAILS ON THE ADR'S OWN TEXT. B argues the in-container setuid wrapper (euid/fsuid 10001) establishes 10001-ownership by mkdir over the RW bind, so "the ADR names no mechanism" is false. Two ADR-017 sentences kill this:
(1) Decision 8 L781-782: the attachment tree is "created **before its first launch**" — i.e. host-side, before any container with fsuid 10001 exists.
(2) Decision 8 L949-951 requires "its source root **and parents**" to be 10001-owned. The parent `MC_HOME/attachments/<session-id>/` is never mounted into the container (only `.../out` is bound at `/mc/attachments/out`); a container process cannot create or chown its own bind's *parent*. Nor can it chown the mount root itself: ADR-019:84 pins `CapDrop=ALL`, so no CAP_CHOWN, and a process cannot chown a file it does not already own. B's mechanism reaches only *children inside* the bind, which is not what the ADR requires. B's own "RESIDUAL" concedes the point it needed to refute ("ADR-017 never names who creates the bind-source root").

WHERE THE FINDING OVERREACHES (agreeing with Verifier A):
1. The uid-10002 half is a reviewer misread. L309-311 "fixes the parent 0555 and the children to the canary-proved final-uid **writable shape**" and table entries "`/mc/session` ... RW" specify *mode*, not owner — reachable by an unprivileged operator process with chmod alone. The finding's premise "cannot chown ... to uid 10001 **or 10002**" is only true of the 10001 half.
2. "Unachievable / permanently dead line" is an empirical prediction the text does not settle. A virtiofsd uid-mapping config or a privileged bootstrap container (the exact shape S1 already proved, spikes/01-setuid-gate/check.sh:49-51, root + named volume) would satisfy the shape — but the ADR names neither. The property is *unnamed*, not proven unrealizable. ADR-019's no-chown sentence is scoped to fallbacks after a red canary, not a general ban on a bootstrap step.
3. Not a spec contradiction. Spec §11.5 (L508) scopes the kernel ownership gate to "The spine's **named volume**"; §16 (L708) says MC_HOME "is protected by ordinary filesystem ownership". The 10001-owned MC_HOME bind is ADR-017's own extension beyond spec, so this is a mechanism gap in a delegated design, not an invariant or spec breach.

NET: CONFIRMED on the narrow claim (the 10001-owned/0700 seal and outbound-attachment roots are required host-side but established by no named actor, and the ADRs foreclose the obvious fillers), REFUTED in its 10002 half, downgraded from "major/permanently dead" to a minor repairable ADR mechanism gap.

DISPOSITION: no test is red (Phase 3 unbuilt; no mc/ Go code touches seals or attachments), nothing in IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md declares it, it breaks no invariant, and it fails closed. Under AGENTS.md §6 log-and-go it belongs in IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md — and is a natural item for the in-flight ADR-020 draft to close by naming a privileged bootstrap step or a uid-mapping requirement. Not Parked; no operator-only input needed.

- docs/adr/017-mount-authorization.md:530 (Decision 6 table): "| `/mc/completion-seal` | exact `MC_HOME/seals/<run-id>/`, Docker RW but privileged-owned mode 0700 | current standalone Worker's setuid completion wrapper only; uid 10002 direct access fails |"

THE ONLY PROVEN MECHANISM IS ROOT-BASED AND NAMED-VOLUME-SCOPED:

SPEC SCOPES THE OWNERSHIP GATE TO THE VOLUME ONLY (so this is a gap in delegated design, not a spec breach):

REFUTING THE FINDING'S 10002 HALF (mode, not owner — chmod suffices):
- docs/adr/017-mount-authorization.md:310-311: "then fixes the parent 0555 and the children to the canary-proved final-uid **writable shape**"
- docs/adr/019-container-resource-envelopes.md:183-185: "a macOS VirtioFS canary at the final `10002:10002` uid performs create, write, fsync, atomic rename, delete ... on the sacrificial bind." — a writability property, reachable without chown.

verdicts.title: ADR-017 Decision 8 requires the host-native surface to read a tree it simultaneously makes unreadable to the host uid
verdicts.reasoning:
Read probe clean and stable (829 lines spec, 965 ADR-016, 1220 ADR-017). I read ADR-017 Decisions 1, 5, 6, 8 and spec §12/§15.5/Inv. 23 directly rather than relying on any prior verdict.

The whole finding turns on one question the three verifiers split on: when Decision 8 says the bind's "source root and parents are owned by the setuid uid at mode 0700," is that a claim about the **host APFS inode** (Verifier C: contradiction) or about the **container-visible mount view** (Verifier B: no contradiction, canary-gated)? The ADR's own text settles it, and it settles it against Verifier B.

DECISIVE: Decision 6's table column is headed literally "**Typed source and access**" — the column describes the *host* source of each bind. Line 502, in that column, reads "exact **operator-owned** mode-0555 task-local skeleton root." That entry is provably a host-side APFS fact, because Decision 5 line 310 says the resident "creates and identity-registers the empty `task-<id>/{source,git}` skeleton **as the host operator**, then fixes the parent 0555 and the children to the canary-proved final-uid writable shape." Line 532, thirty lines down in the *same column with the same grammar*, reads "`MC_HOME/attachments/<session-id>/out`, Docker RW but **privileged-uid mode 0700**." One column, one grammar, one view. Verifier B's rescue — that the 0700 claim is "a mount-view statement, not a claim about the macOS inode's owner" — requires the same column to silently switch views between line 502 and line 532. The text does not support that.

Decision 5 line 310 is doubly damaging to the container-view reading: it shows the ADR's actual mental model is *uid-faithful host semantics* — ownership stays with the host operator, and the container's final uid is granted access by tuning **modes** ("fixes the parent 0555 ... to the canary-proved final-uid writable shape"). The unknown the canary resolves is which *mode* the final uid needs, not whether ownership is presented faithfully.

Verifier B's other pillar also fails on inspection. The canary's phrase "same filesystem, Docker share, **ownership**, and ACL regime as configured `MC_HOME`" is a *test-fidelity* clause — run the scratch canary under the same regime as the real MC_HOME, don't test in /tmp. It is not an admission that container-visible ownership may diverge from host-visible ownership. Reading "ownership ... regime" as "we do not assume uid faithfulness" is an inference the sentence does not carry, and it is contradicted by line 310's explicit host-operator-ownership model.

With the host-side reading established, the contradictions are text-decidable and need no platform knowledge:

1. AGAINST THE SPEC. Spec §15.5 requires "the native surface reads the file directly." Spec §12 fixes that surface as an "ordinary **per-user LaunchAgent** in the operator's GUI session," under Inv. 23 ("ordinary host processes ... not containers"). A host directory at mode 0700 owned by uid 10001 denies traversal to the operator uid. So the ADR makes unreachable exactly what §15.5 mandates.

2. NO ROAD OUT, BY THE ADR'S OWN WORDS. Decision 8: "there is no fallback that **changes ownership, widens modes/ACLs**..." — no ACL grant to the operator uid is authorized. And there is no host-side privilege road: `grep` over ADR-017 returns **zero** hits for `chown`, `sudo`, or root — nothing on the host can chown an inode to uid 10001 in the first place, and spec §12 forecloses LaunchDaemons.

3. INTERNAL CONTRADICTION, PLATFORM-INDEPENDENT. "source root **and parents**" reaches `MC_HOME/attachments/<session-id>/`, `MC_HOME/attachments/`, and `MC_HOME` itself. Decision 1 independently requires a "non-symlink **operator-owned** `MC_HOME`" and rejects on "Another owner." Decision 8 and Decision 1 cannot both hold. This is ADR-vs-ADR, decidable from the text alone, with no VirtioFS fact needed.

4. THE INBOUND ARM DIES TOO. If the parent `<session-id>/` is 10001/0700, the operator-uid surface cannot even *create* under the sibling `in/staging` — yet the same canary paragraph requires "the host-native surface publishes a new inbound object," and §15.5 requires "the native surface writes the bytes onto the file plane."

I considered Verifier A's position (UNVERIFIABLE) seriously — it is the most careful of the three — but it grants the container-view reading as "available," and the Decision 6 column grammar plus Decision 5 line 310 close that off. Once the reading is fixed by the ADR's own text, the finding is decidable without adjudicating Docker Desktop's uid presentation, which is exactly the platform fact A correctly said is absent from the repo (`docs/priors/` holds only keychain, claude-sdk, codex-proxy notes; no VirtioFS/ownership prior).

Even the narrowest possible reading of "parents" (only `out/` and below) does not save it: 0700-on-`out` alone kills the canary's outbound arm and §15.5's direct read.

SCOPE HONESTY: this is a design-document defect, not shipped behavior. No Go code under `mc/` implements the attachment plane's ownership/mode logic, and IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md declares no deviation on this point. Per the task's stated rule, an ADR whose text contradicts the spec is CONFIRMED even with no code behind it.

DISPOSITION: fix-red-first — amend ADR-017 before Phase 3 builds the attachment plane. The conservative repair is available inside delegated ADR authority and does not need the operator: keep the host inodes operator-owned (as Decision 5 line 310 already does for the task tree), and obtain the uid-10002 denial from the mount/mode shape rather than from a chown to 10001 that no host process can perform. What must change is the ADR's text: it must say which view its ownership claims describe, and Decision 8's "source root and parents" must stop colliding with Decision 1's operator-owned MC_HOME.

verdicts.title: ADR-016 forbids the helper the MC_HOME bind that §16.4 requires it to hold for backups, and ADR-019 has no class for a separate backup container
verdicts.reasoning:
The finding is a disjunction and both branches are refuted by the text.

BRANCH 1 — "ADR-016 forbids the helper the MC_HOME bind that §16.4 requires." False. ADR-016 Decision 1 (lines 85-87) is a two-sentence pair: a general exclusion followed immediately by its one explicit carve-out. The reviewer quoted both sentences but treated only the first as operative. The ADR does not forbid the backups bind — it names it as the single permitted MC_HOME mount. This is exactly what spec §16.4 requires ("the helper's bind-mounted MC_HOME/backups/ path"), so the ADR and spec agree rather than contradict.

The sub-claim "a warm long-lived helper cannot acquire a bind per-verb" is technically true of Docker but does not describe the ADR. The ADR says "its FIXED backup-target bind" — fixed means established at container creation, not acquired at verb time. "Separately" modifies the bind's relationship to the spine volume (in addition to it), not its timing. Spec §11.5 (line 516) confirms the helper is created whole and "can be killed and recreated at any moment" — mounts are set at creation, and docs/phase3-contract.md:413 puts helper creation in the `container` onboarding section. The sentence scopes AUTHORITY (only the mc backup verb consumes that bind), not dynamic mount acquisition.

Corroborating the intended scope, ADR-016's rejected-alternatives list (line 954) states the non-goal as "Mount Docker, all host roots, or `MC_HOME` into the helper" — i.e. mounting ALL of MC_HOME, exposing "credential/config roots". The narrow backups target is not a credential/config root; it is the sanctioned exception.

BRANCH 2 — "If the ADR instead means a separate short-lived backup container, ADR-019 has no backup class." This conditional's antecedent is false. docs/phase3-contract.md:414 states backup crosses THE HELPER. No separate backup container exists anywhere in the design, so ADR-019's six-class list needs no `backup` entry: backup runs inside the helper, which HAS a class tuple (ADR-019 Decision 1, line 27: helper 500m / 512 MiB / 128). The resource-bounds concern dissolves — the helper is bounded, and nothing unbounded or undispatchable results.

I also checked ADR-017's mention of "backup" (line 250). It is unrelated: MC_HOME backup roots are listed in the non-subtractable protected set governing Worksource/agent mount authorization, not helper composition. It does not bear on the helper's own mounts.

Code check: no shipped code contradicts this. mc/cmd/mc/cli_test.go:3961-3962 asserts snapshots land under MC_HOME/backups/ via a single path with no separate container. IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md declares no deviation here, and none is needed.

RESIDUAL: read in strict isolation, "mounts ... nothing from MC_HOME" is loose absolute phrasing whose exception lives in the next sentence. That is ordinary prose economy with the qualifier adjacent, not a defect. Rewording is optional at best; I would not spend a change on it.

"The helper mounts the spine named volume and nothing from `MC_HOME` or the host credential/file plane. `mc backup` may separately receive only its fixed backup-target bind."

"- **Mount Docker, all host roots, or `MC_HOME` into the helper.** Expands the privileged helper and exposes credential/config roots."

verdicts.title: ADR-017 makes /workspace read-only for standalone-task roles, contradicting §11.3 without declaring the deviation
verdicts.reasoning:
Both halves of the finding check out against the literal text.

1. The ADR really does flip the row. ADR-017 Decision 6's destination table binds `/workspace` as "exact operator-owned mode-0555 task-local skeleton root, always RO" for standalone-task roles. Decision 5 reinforces that this is deliberate and structural ("The task root has one registered identity, is operator-owned mode 0555 ... Thus uid 10002 cannot chmod the parent or create a sibling"), and the setup table repeats it ("setup `/repo/task` | exact mode-0555 task-local skeleton root, always RO"). Spec §11.3's mount table fixes the same container path the other way: "`/workspace` | container-local scratch | RW". This is not a reviewer misreading — the ADR row is explicit about "always RO", and the scope ("standalone-task roles use the bind") covers exactly Worker/Verifier/Packager/Refiner, which the ADR's own acceptance section names.

2. The deviation is genuinely undeclared. ADR-017's only deviation declaration (line 413) reads "This topology is an explicit, conservative deviation from §§6.2, 7, and 11.1" — §11.3 is absent. The matching IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md entry ("2026-07-13 — Standalone tasks use a sanitized task-local Git repository") likewise scopes itself to "spec §5, §6.2, §7, §11.1" and its "Spec impact" line names only "§§6.2, 7, and 11.1". No entry in IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md mentions the `/workspace` access-mode change; grep for "scratch" in the ADR hits only canary/probe test infrastructure (lines 969, 1164), never the agent scratch semantics §11.3 assigns to that row.

Two things temper this to *minor* rather than major, and I want to be precise about them rather than inflate the finding:

- The ADR's header does list §11.3 among sections that delegate to it, but the delegated scope is stated narrowly: "require an operator-owned allowlist, an extend-only blocked floor, bilateral RW consent, and two enumerated cross-Worksource RO profiles **without fixing their grammar or collision-free container destinations**." The access mode of a row §11.3 explicitly fixes is not "grammar" or "destination", so the header does not license the flip. It also cannot substitute for a deviation entry, which AGENTS.md §6 requires.

- The *capability* §11.3 wanted is not destroyed, only relocated without saying so. The ADR preserves RW at `/workspace/artifacts/<artifact-target>/<suffix>`, `/mc/records/output` ("RW | current authorized output-writing pipeline run only"), and, for the Verifier specifically, an "execution-scoped sealed-tree materialization overlaid RW". And for non-standalone roles the ADR preserves the spec's semantics exactly — "`/workspace` for non-standalone roles ... are rootfs directories", i.e. RW container-local scratch. So this is a documentation/logging defect over a narrow, arguably-correct design change, not a broken system. But the finding's actual claim — the row is flipped, the flip is real, and §11.3 is not named in either deviation record — is supported by the text, and the ADR nowhere restates where standalone roles' container-local scratch lands.

No shipped Go code contradicts either way: grep for `/workspace` under mc/ hits only e2e test fixtures using an older `--workspace-root /workspace/source` shape, no mount-plan implementation. This is an ADR-text finding, which the task rules explicitly permit to confirm.

verdicts.title: Inv. 26 / trace-projection resolution verified: it holds
verdicts.reasoning:
Finding #10 is an info-note asserting that the ledger's claim ("the Inv. 26 tension was resolved conservatively") actually checks out rather than being merely asserted. Read cold, the text supports the note on every axis, and every quote in its evidence block is verbatim accurate against the files.

1. The authorizing grant is real and independent. Spec §15.3 (line 635) grants the operator-scope Homie "read-only mounts across all Worksources' workspaces, session files, and artifacts," and Inv. 22 (line 39) names this as one of exactly two declared read-only exceptions. So Homie visibility into pipeline session *files* is spec-granted, not an ADR invention. The residual byte-visibility that a same-inode hardlink creates is precisely what §15.3 authorizes.

2. Inv. 26's literal requirement is a statement about the *folder mount*, and the ADR does not perform that mount. Inv. 26 (line 43) says each run "owns one trace-only session folder on the file plane, created before its container starts and mounted only into that container"; §11.3's mount table (line 461) says "Each container mounts **only its own** folder." ADR-017 Decision 7 (lines 700-702, 714-715) keeps that literal: the run's own folder is RW only into that run, "The original `sessions/<id>/` folder is never mounted, and no trace entry is a Docker mount."

3. The projection is strictly narrower than the §15.3 grant on all three claimed axes. Finalized-only (Decision 7 eligibility gate requires "finalized immutable locators and terminal writer state" and no live lease/writer/container); pipeline-only (lines 764-765 exclude Homie-owned traces, with a sound stated reason at lines 1209-1210: resume can append to their inode and unlink cannot revoke an already-open fd in a warm Homie); no folder mount (line 714). The §15.3 grant is broader than all three, so the ADR under-claims its own authorization — the conservative direction.

4. Decision 8's attachment plane sits outside the invariant's scope entirely: line 991, "Neither direction lives under `sessions/`, so the trace-only invariant remains literal," and the tree is rooted at `MC_HOME/attachments/<session-id>/`, not `sessions/`.

5. The deviation is already declared, satisfying AGENTS.md §6's log-and-go rule. IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md:683-699 names the exact gap, the choice, and "Needs your decision: no" — consistent with the finding's cross-reference.

The one point worth naming honestly: a hardlink does make finalized pipeline trace *bytes* readable from a second container, so an aggressive reading of Inv. 26's intent ("one run's verbatim record is invisible to every other run," line 461) is in tension. But that clause is scoped to other *runs* reading each other, and §15.3 carves the operator-scope Homie out of exactly that scoping as a declared exception. The ADR also avoids the alternatives that would genuinely break things (copy/reflink would create a second unredacted byte store; symlinks re-expose source traversal; per-trace mounts exhaust the 256-mount bound) — reasoning stated at lines 1204-1208. This is the conservative option under AGENTS.md §6's definition: it preserves the invariants, deviates least from the spec's text, and is easy to reverse if §15.3 is clarified.

Verdict CONFIRMED means the info-note's claim is accurate — not that a defect exists. There is no defect here.
verdicts.evidence:
SPEC §15.3 (specs/mission-control-spec.md:635) — the authorizing grant:
"**Confinement.** Homies run containerized like everything else, under a dedicated **operator-scope profile**: read-only mounts across all Worksources' workspaces, session files, and artifacts, plus board-wide reads via `mc get/list`. This is one of two declared read-only exceptions to Inv. 22's per-Worksource scoping (the other is the Strategist(propose) seeding-scope pass, §3/§11.1) — justified because the session is filesystem-read-only and answers to the operator, whose own jurisdiction spans all Worksources by definition."

SPEC Inv. 22 (line 39): "Two read-only exceptions are explicitly declared and enumerated, both filesystem-read-only, mounting several Worksources only to read: the operator-scope Homie (§15) …"

SPEC Inv. 26 (line 43): "Each run — pipeline role or Homie session alike — owns one trace-only session folder on the file plane, created before its container starts and mounted only into that container; the harness writes its native session record directly into it as it runs. Session folders are never swept…"

SPEC §11.3 mount table (line 461): "**trace-only** (Inv. 26): the harness's native session file lands here durably as it is written. Each container mounts **only its own** folder — one run's verbatim record is invisible to every other run."

ADR-017 Decision 7 (docs/adr/017-mount-authorization.md:700-702): "A run's own `sessions/<run_id>/` directory is mounted RW only into that run; a Homie resume remounts its own folder. No other container gets that directory."

ADR-017 Decision 7 (lines 714-716): "The original `sessions/<id>/` folder is never mounted, and no trace entry is a Docker mount. Thus history growth does not consume the 256-mount plan bound."

ADR-017 Decision 7 (lines 704-705) — pipeline-only + finalized-only: "A Homie gets **one** RO bind of a derived, owner-keyed hardlink tree containing only finalized pipeline-run traces"

ADR-017 Decision 7 (lines 763-770): "Pipeline runs never resume, so an eligible inode can never become a live writer later. **Homie-owned native traces are never linked into this shared projection**, even while their rows are ended/reaped: those sessions are resumable, and unlinking would not revoke an fd already opened by another warm Homie. … This matches §15.3's cross-Worksource exception: pipeline runs belong to Worksources; Homie sessions do not."

ADR-017 rationale (lines 1204-1210): "Mounting the sessions tree or each completed trace into Homie either breaks Inv. 26 or eventually exhausts the finite plan. Copying/reflinking traces makes a second unredacted byte store; symlinks re-expose source-directory traversal. The one same-inode, owner-keyed hardlink projection preserves a single byte record and a fixed mount count for immutable pipeline traces. IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md:683-699 — deviation already declared:
"## 2026-07-13 — Homie historical trace access preserves folder exclusivity
- Gap: Inv. 26 says every run's trace-only session folder is mounted only into its owning container, while §15.3 requires an operator-scope Homie to receive read-only mounts across session files. Mounting the whole sessions tree would violate the invariant and give a live file a second directory alias.
- Choice: keep each folder owner-exclusive and give a Homie only enumerated, completed native trace **files** as individual read-only references under a deterministic operator-reference tree. … This satisfies the later file-access clause while preserving the named invariant and is easy to replace if the spec is clarified.
- Spec impact: §15.3 should distinguish individual historical file mounts from session-directory mounts.
- Needs your decision: no"

verdicts.title: ADR-018 asserts separate user namespaces, which would break its own `meta skuid` enforcement and contradicts the pinned target
verdicts.reasoning:
CONFIRMED on the narrow textual claim, but the finding's framing and severity overstate what the text supports.

WHAT IS REAL. ADR-018 twice states that the guard's and agent's user namespaces "remain separate." On the pinned target that is false: implementation-handoff.md line 236 makes "no user-namespace remap" a permanent canary, and the trusted spike evidence (spikes/01-setuid-gate/RESULT.md assertion 8) proves an identity `/proc/self/uid_map` of `0 0 4294967295` — the init user namespace, shared by every container. So the containers are in the SAME user namespace, not separate ones. Worse, the ADR's own mechanism depends on that sharing: `meta skuid` munges the socket's kuid through the user namespace owning the network namespace, so an agent in a genuinely separate userns with a different map would munge uid 10002 to overflowuid and no owner predicate could ever match. Read literally, ADR-018's "user ... namespaces remain separate" and "every owner predicate is an nftables `meta skuid` match against the socket's kernel credential" are jointly unrealizable. Per the review rules ("CONFIRMED if the ADR's text really does contradict the spec/invariant or is internally unrealizable"), that clears the bar. No deviation is declared in IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md, and no Go code under mc/ mentions user namespaces at all, so this is purely an ADR-text issue.

WHAT DOES NOT SURVIVE. (1) The finding calls this the mechanism "breaking." It does not. The sentence's function is to enumerate what `NetworkMode=container:` does NOT share; the same list includes "capability namespaces," which is not a Linux namespace type at all, showing the list is loose isolation-posture prose, not a topology specification. The ADR nowhere *requires* creating separate user namespaces, and Docker offers no per-container userns absent daemon-wide userns-remap, which the handoff forbids. No implementer would enable remap because of this sentence. The design is correct; the description of it is wrong.
(2) The Decision 8 half of the finding is refuted as a spec contradiction. Spec §11.7 explicitly scopes the remap/ECI diagnostic to `mc doctor` and onboarding, not to ADR-018's per-spawn preclaim proof. Decision 8 omitting it is therefore a gap in one ADR's diagnostics, not a contradiction of the spec's assignment of that duty.
(3) The claimed security impact is refuted by the finding's own evidence, which concedes the guard would "wedge" — i.e. fail closed. A wedged guard lands squarely in Decision 8's already-enumerated "actual guard cannot reach ready" class with the correct consequence (deployment health, no claim, no task charge). No invariant is breached and there is no fail-open; the real cost is an unhelpful error message where the spec would have named the cause.

NET. A genuine prose defect worth a small ADR correction — strike "user" from both lists (and ideally state affirmatively that the shared init user namespace is precisely what makes `meta skuid` meaningful), and optionally add uid-remap/ECI to Decision 8's deployment-health causes for diagnostic parity with §11.7. Not a design defect, not a security defect, not major.

verdicts.title: ADR-019's per-class ranges have no aggregate budget check; a fully in-range config exceeds the pinned VM and is classified as infrastructure failure, not config health
verdicts.reasoning:
The finding's quotes are all verbatim-accurate and its arithmetic is correct (6144+1024+4096+1024+1024 = 13,312 MiB), and it is literally true that ADR-019 specifies no aggregate sum check. But the asserted consequence — "a fully in-range config exceeds the pinned VM and is classified as infrastructure failure, not config health" — fails at three independent joints, each refutable from the text.

(1) The pin is a FLOOR, not a capacity. Handoff line 458 reads "≥4 CPU / ≥8 GB to the VM" — a minimum requirement the operator must meet, not the VM's size. This is the only VM-size text in specs/ or docs/adr/ (grep confirms exactly two hits: handoff:458 and ADR-019:6, which restates it as "≥4 CPU/≥8 GiB"). A config whose ceilings sum to 13 GiB does not "exceed the pinned VM"; an operator raising every class to maximum on a 32 GB VM violates nothing. The finding silently converts "≥8 GB" into "= 8 GB."

(2) The values are ceilings, not reservations, so the sum is not an allocation. ADR-019 Decision 2 is explicit that it sets hard ceilings and deliberately does NOT set reservations: "CPU shares and memory reservations are not substitutes for ceilings." `Memory = memory_mib × 1048576` is a cgroup `memory.max` — a cap on what a container MAY use, not memory taken at start. Over-committing ceilings past physical RAM is the normal, near-universal Docker posture, not a misconfiguration; nothing is reserved, so there is nothing to exceed. A sum-of-ceilings check is only meaningful against reservations, which the ADR pointedly does not use. The premise that in-range ⇒ OOM does not follow.

(3) The rejected alternative is about DERIVATION, not validation, so "deliberately rejects any capacity-aware validation" overstates the text. "Dynamic percentages of current VM capacity make plans non-replayable" rejects computing limit VALUES from live capacity (which would make an immutable plan resolve differently on replay — consistent with Decision 1's "Each immutable plan carries the fully resolved tuple"). It says nothing about validating a static tuple, and a static sum check against the ≥8 GiB floor is not excluded by that line.

The Decision 4 mis-classification claim also fails on its own terms. Decision 4 reserves config health for a parseable defect — "an explicit invalid/unbounded tuple is configuration health" — enumerated as "Unknown classes/keys, duplicate values, zero/negative values, `-1`, strings such as `unlimited|max|4g`, and out-of-range integers." An in-range tuple is by construction none of these: it is valid and bounded. And if a machine genuinely exhausts RAM, "An OOM ... is infrastructure failure" is the correct classification, not a mis-routing — the memory ran out, which is a machine fact. The finding needs the OOM to be a config defect wearing an infra label, but under (1) and (2) the config is not defective. "Unbounded failure loop" is asserted with no supporting ADR text and cuts against Decision 2's stated reaping path ("ordinary heartbeat/deadline reaping classifies the run as infrastructure") and Decision 4's "no new claim and no task charge/block."

Finally, the shipped defaults are conservative even by the finding's own over-strict metric: 4096+512+2048+512+512 = 7680 MiB, under 8 GiB as a sum of ceilings. The scenario requires an operator to deliberately max every class, on a machine sized at exactly the documented floor, with every workload simultaneously consuming its full ceiling.

The spec §11.1 concurrency quote is accurate and the classes cited can indeed be live together — that part of the finding holds. But concurrency of ceilings is not contention of allocations. No spec text or invariant requires an aggregate budget check (grep over the spec for budget/capacity/limit language surfaces only task-level budgets: `dispatch_retries`, `correction_count`, `[budgets]` throughput/quality — nothing about container memory aggregates). No deviation is declared in IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md, and none is needed. This is a reviewer misreading — the floor read as a cap, and ceilings read as reservations — not an ADR/spec contradiction.

verdicts.title: ADR-019 pins a 256-PID range floor for the class that must run Chromium, with no spike evidence that Playwright fits under a task-counting `pids.max`
verdicts.reasoning:
The finding's individual quotes are all accurate. ADR-019 does pin `pipeline` PIDs at a 256–1024 operator range, does set `PidsLimit = pids` / `pids.max`, and does require the S8 Chromium/Playwright canary to run at the minimum envelope. spikes/08-arm64-image/RESULT.md really does record only a bare Chromium launch (A6) with no PidsLimit applied and no process/thread count — so it is true that no spike evidences 256 tasks, and the underlying kernel fact (the cgroup pids controller counts tasks/threads, not processes) is correct. That much is real.

What is not supported is the finding's actual claim: that the ADR is therefore internally unrealizable and "the decision this ADR exists to pin is not actually made."

1. No spec contradiction exists. I grepped both specs: the only hit for "pids|PidsLimit|fork|EAGAIN" across specs/mission-control-spec.md and specs/implementation-handoff.md is an unrelated line about role directives. The handoff mentions "resource bounds" (line 393) as a Phase-3 requirement and fixes only Docker Desktop's ≥4 CPU / ≥8 GB VM floor (line 458). There is no spec text, invariant, or handoff clause on PID limits for the ADR to contradict. The ADR's own preamble states this correctly and is not overreaching: the spec "supplies no per-container values or override grammar."

2. The ADR never claims S8 evidences 256. The finding attributes a claim the document does not make. ADR-019 cites S8 only for the browser image and the `--no-sandbox` flag ("the spec fixes ... S8's browser image"), which is exactly what RESULT.md supports. There is no misrepresentation of evidence to confirm.

3. The finding's own closing quote refutes it. It quotes the escape hatch — "If a minimum cannot pass its mechanism canary, the ADR minimum is raised; the test is never bypassed and the limit is never removed" — and reads it as proof the decision is unmade. It is the opposite: that sentence *is* a decision, and a fail-closed one. The ADR decides (a) the starting floor, (b) that the floor is tested at the minimum rather than assumed, and (c) the exact resolution if the test fails, with the invariant-preserving direction fixed (raise the floor; never remove the limit, never bypass the test). An ADR that sets a value which a pending acceptance test then validates is a normal, correct TDD design artifact, not an unrealizable one. Under this ADR a >256-task Chromium produces a red canary and a raised minimum — the designed outcome, not a broken design.

4. The ADR anticipates the symptom deliberately. The finding cites "PID exhaustion may surface only as `fork/clone` `EAGAIN` and need not terminate the container" as evidence the ADR "anticipates the symptom without pricing it into the range." But that clause is doing failure *classification* work, not range-setting work — it exists so a silent EAGAIN gets reaped as infrastructure rather than scored as a quality verdict. Pricing the range is what the minimum-envelope canary does.

No code implements this yet (no `pids`/`PidsLimit` hits anywhere under mc/), and no deviation is declared in IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md — correctly, since nothing deviates. The residual engineering datum (256 tasks is plausibly tight for headless Chromium + node + Playwright) is a real thing the S8-at-minimum canary will discover, which is precisely why the ADR mandates that canary. Nothing to fix in the ADR text.

verdicts.title: ADR-018's mandatory five-container preclaim probe makes a spawn tick outlast the spec's stated reap-latency bound
verdicts.reasoning:
The finding's quoted texts all exist verbatim and the tension is real in the documents' own words.

1. ADR-018 Decision 8 (/Users/vinchenkov/Documents/dev/ai/homie/docs/adr/018-network-guard-gateway.md:394-396) does mandate five containers plus a bridge per spawn/wake candidate, with create/inspect/start/stop/remove-and-confirm-absent sequencing, and makes it unskippable even when a read-only policy calculation succeeds.

2. ADR-018 Decision 6 (same file, ~lines 258-262) does place that Docker probe inside a one-use control channel whose non-inventory wall allowance is 120 seconds — explicitly twice the spec's tick_interval_s — and that channel is created "For each resident tick" around ADR-016's short-lived `mc dispatch` broker child, i.e. the probe runs inside the tick.

3. specs/mission-control-spec.md:751 states the reap-latency bound as "threshold + one tick" with tick_interval_s = 60, and §10 (line 334) states a firing landing while a tick is in flight is "skipped, never queued or run concurrently". Those two together only yield "threshold + one tick" if a tick fits inside 60s. ADR-018's own budget explicitly permits a compliant, successful spawn tick to consume up to 120s, at which point at least one subsequent firing is skipped and the stated bound no longer holds. The same applies to §7's "landing latency is at most one tick" (line 226) and Inv. 3's "up-to-one-tick latency ... is accepted" (line 20).

4. I confirmed the negative half of the claim: grep across docs/adr/018 and docs/adr/016 finds no restatement or relaxation of tick_interval_s or the reap bound (ADR-018's only other `tick` mentions are the crash-sweep at line 480 and reconciliation at line 514), and IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md declares no deviation on tick duration vs. probe budget (its only nearby timing entry, line 262, is about spawn_grace_s vs docker-run latency, a different concern).

Honest scoping of what is NOT established: 120s is a timeout ceiling, not an asserted expected duration — the ADR never claims the probe takes 120s. And the spec never independently guarantees that any tick completes within tick_interval_s (base-spec ticks already start containers and wire proxies), so the "threshold + one tick" line was arguably always an approximation. The confirmed defect is therefore narrower than "the probe always blows the bound": it is that ADR-018's text explicitly sanctions a worst case that exceeds the spec's stated bound, on a mandatory unskippable path, without restating or relaxing that bound anywhere. That is a genuine design-document contradiction, not a reviewer misreading — the reviewer's title slightly overstates ("makes a spawn tick outlast") what is really "permits a spawn tick to outlast".

Invariant contact: Inv. 3's normative core (one dispatcher, one action per tick) is preserved by ADR-018; only its descriptive latency clause is strained. Nothing fails closed or unsafely. Hence minor, and log-and-go per AGENTS.md §6 rather than parking.

verdicts.title: ADR-019 calls the network guard "credential-free"; ADR-018 gives it a 32-byte runtime launch credential
verdicts.reasoning:
Read probe passed cleanly (829 lines spec, 965 lines ADR-016; no flapping). Both halves of the finding's evidence are verbatim accurate — ADR-019 line 40 does contain "credential-free" and ADR-018 does hand the guard a 32-byte mlocked launch credential. The finding nonetheless fails on what "credential-free" refers to.

1. The reviewer's evidence truncates the governing context. The quoted sentence is the second of a pair whose subject is *which components get a Docker resource class*: "The resident-hosted credential gateway is native (§12), so it has no Docker class here. `network-guard` is only ADR-018's credential-free runtime-side namespace/firewall/relay." "credential-free" is an anaphoric contrast to "credential gateway" in the immediately prior clause — it disambiguates the table row from the gateway component. It is not an assertion that the guard holds zero bytes anyone calls a credential.

2. That reading is what the spec's own term of art supports. Spec §11 treats "credential gateway" as the provider-secret-injecting component ("same mounts, same egress policy, same credential gateway, no new secret surface"). ADR-018 confirms the guard is credential-free in exactly that sense: it "mounts no Worksource, spine, session, runtime control directory, CA key, injection table, or runtime socket." The 32-byte launch credential is an authenticator *to* the gateway, not the credential material the gateway manages — ADR-018 keeps these planes distinct.

3. The claimed consequence is contradicted by the ADR text. The finding says "an implementer reading ADR-019's class definition as the guard's contract would omit the credential plane." But ADR-019's sentence says the guard "is **only ADR-018's** ... namespace/firewall/relay" — an explicit delegation pointing the implementer *at* ADR-018, not away from it. ADR-019 Decision 1 defines three positive integers ("Resource configuration is three positive integers"); the class table sets CPU/memory/PIDs. Nothing in ADR-019 purports to define the guard's credential contract, so nothing there can be mistaken for one.

4. No spec text or invariant is contradicted at all. Even taken at face value this is an ADR-to-ADR word choice, not a spec conflict — which is the bar the review was scoped to.

Checks performed: `credential-free` appears exactly once in the repo outside the review JSON itself (ADR-019 line 40); IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md declares no deviation on this; no code under mc/ mentions netguard/network-guard, so no shipped behavior is implicated.

Residual, offered as an editorial note rather than a defect: ADR-018 does use the noun "credential" for the 32 bytes ("plus the private one-shot launch credential"), so ADR-019's adjective is doing double duty against a sibling ADR's vocabulary. A one-word tightening ("gateway-free", or "provider-credential-free") would remove the ambiguity that produced this finding. That is optional polish; the current text is correct in context and misleads no implementer.

verdicts.title: ADR-019 attributes its machine-budget premise to the spec, which contains no CPU/memory floor
verdicts.reasoning:
The finding's factual core holds on the actual text.

1. ADR-019's header does attribute the machine-budget figure to "the spec". Its own sentence explicitly contrasts the two documents ("the Phase-3 handoff requires resource bounds; **the spec** fixes Docker Desktop ≥4 CPU/≥8 GiB"), so "the spec" cannot be charitably read as loose shorthand for the specs/ suite — the sentence names the handoff separately in the same clause.

2. The spec contains no CPU or memory figure at all. `grep -c` over specs/mission-control-spec.md returns **0 occurrences of "GiB"**, and 0 for "GB", "CPU", and "cpu". The only "memory" hits are prose ("dedupe memory", "in-memory queue") — none is RAM sizing. The spec's §16.3 `[container]` knob table (lines 757–765) lists exactly five knobs — container runtime, base image tag, package-cache root, mount allowlist path, additional blocked patterns — and no resource values, exactly as the finding states.

3. The real source is specs/implementation-handoff.md line 458, an operator-input row, and it reads **GB**, not GiB — so the ADR also carries a unit slip alongside the misattribution.

4. The premise is load-bearing, not decorative: ADR-019's range table ceilings (pipeline max 4000m / 6144 MiB) are plainly sized against a 4-CPU / 8-GiB host budget, so the table's justifying authority is the misattributed one.

5. The sibling ADRs establish the convention the header breaks: ADR-016 ("§§10–12 place…"), ADR-017 ("§§5, 11.1–11.3, 15.3, and 16 require…"), and ADR-018 ("§11.4 fixes…") all cite the spec by section number. ADR-019 cites "the spec" for a figure that has no section to cite because the text does not exist there.

Nothing refutes it: IMPLEMENTATION-NOTES.md declares no deviation on this point (grep for "GiB|GB|4 CPU|misattrib|traceab|019" finds no relevant entry).

Scope discipline: this is a citation/traceability defect in one header line, not a substantive design error. The numbers themselves are correct against the handoff, no invariant is breached, and no code depends on the header prose. Two of the header's three attributions are defensible on the merits (the spec does fix setuid preconditions at line 508 and the browser image at line 441, even though labeling them "S1's"/"S8's" borrows spike names from the handoff). Only the ≥4 CPU/≥8 GiB clause is sourced to a document that does not contain it. Hence CONFIRMED at info severity, not major.
